title: Chapter 32: The Gathering Storm
content: Three weeks outside the estate changed everything.

Song Yilan recovered in a private clinic that Liu's network operated—off the books, array-shielded, staffed by practitioners who understood dimensional medicine. The synthetic enhancement was slowly being reversed; her cardiac function was stabilizing, and her channeling capability was decreasing as the artificial amplification faded. Within six months, she'd be a normal woman with a minor bloodline sensitivity and no memory of the portal.
The Patriarch, meanwhile, was accelerating.

Feng's intelligence from inside the estate painted a grim picture: with Song Yilan gone, the Patriarch had lost his synthetic channeler. Without a channeler, the dual-frequency tuning couldn't proceed. Without the tuning, the containment cascade was eroding his conversion progress day by day.

So the Patriarch had pivoted. Instead of trying to stop the cascade, he was *racing* it—converting the remaining unconverted nodes as fast as possible, before the cascade could drift them back toward containment. He'd found a new channeler—a young man from a minor bloodline, hastily trained, capable of basic frequency output but nothing approaching Song Yilan's level.
It wouldn't be enough for a full activation. But it might be enough for one node.

"The Obsidian Tablet—Node 6," Liu reported during a strategy session at the safe house. "The Patriarch is using the new channeler to reinforce Node 6's conversion. If he can stabilize it against the cascade, he'll have five confirmed nodes and two unaccounted for."
"Node 4 is locked in my mother's vault," Xue said. "He can't touch it."
"Node 7 is locked at Mount Heng," Ren added. "The shrine keepers are maintaining the containment seal."
"Which means the Patriarch needs all five remaining converted nodes to attempt activation—which would fail, because the seven-pointed star requires all seven vertices. But—" Liu hesitated.
"But?"

"The Patriarch has been researching. The original containment practitioners included a failsafe: a mechanism to *re-tune* the star's geometry. With five nodes properly calibrated, a pentagonal configuration could theoretically create a smaller but functional aperture. Not the full breach—something more controlled, more targeted. But still... a door."
*A pentagonal aperture.* Not the end-of-the-world portal the entity wanted, but a door nonetheless. And even a small door could let something through.
"How long does he have?"
"At his current acceleration? Six weeks. Maybe less if the new channeler proves more capable than expected."
Six weeks. The clock was no longer counting down in years—it was counting down in weeks.

"We need to go back," Xue said. "Into the estate. While the Patriarch is focused on Node 6, we access the other converted nodes and push the containment drift past the point of no return. If we can drift even one more node across the 5% threshold, the cascade will pull it the rest of the way automatically."
"And the Patriarch will be waiting," Ren said. "He knows we're coming. He's probably been preparing countermeasures since we left."

"Then we don't go in as enemies. We go in as family." Xue touched the jade pendant. "I'm still his daughter. Feng is still his son. And the array recognizes bloodline authority—if we enter through the front door, with full family protocol, the security systems won't flag us as hostile."
"Until your father gives the order to arrest us."

"By then, we'll be inside the restricted wing. And inside the restricted wing, bloodline authority trumps security protocols." She looked at Ren. "My father built this system to keep enemies out. He never imagined that the enemies would be his own children."
It was bold. It was risky. It was exactly the kind of plan that a woman who'd spent twelve years navigating her father's power structures would devise—not a military assault, but a bloodline siege.

"I'm in," Feng said. He'd joined them from the estate two days ago—slipping out during a shift change, carrying the jade token and a head full of intelligence. "I can get us through the ceremony hall. My bloodline key still works."

"And I can create a frequency mask for the approach," Liu added. "The Patriarch's array surveillance will see three bloodline signatures entering the estate—which is exactly what they'll expect, because you're family. But they won't see the fourth signature." He looked at Ren. "You'll need to channel the mask. Can you do it with 2.3%?"
"I can enhance the mask beyond that," Ren said. "The containment cascade has been feeding me. Current power: 2.9% and climbing."

*2.9%.* Nearly 3%—the threshold needed for a spore purge, and more importantly, enough power to interface meaningfully with the containment array. If he could reach 3% before the operation, he'd have enough juice for two major channeling events: one to push Node 2 past the 5% drift threshold, and one to reinforce Node 5's containment restoration.
It would cost him nearly everything. But the math worked.
"Then we go in seven days," Ren said. "Time enough for me to reach 3%, for Liu to prepare the frequency mask, and for Feng to re-enter the estate and establish a clean exit route."
"And my father's pentagonal aperture?"
"Six weeks minimum. Seven days won't change the timeline significantly. But it will give us enough power to make a decisive move."

The team nodded—Xue, Feng, Liu, Hong (who'd been running logistics from the safe house), and Ren. Five people against a Patriarch with forty years of preparation, four converted nodes, and an interdimensional entity pulling strings from beyond reality.
*Outnumbered, outgunned, outmaneuvered,* Ren thought. *Just the way I like it.*
Seven days passed.

The containment cascade fed Ren's power relentlessly, each node's drift sending energy back through the dimensional network and into his cells. By day six, he was at 3.2%—enough for two major channeling events with a thin margin for error.
By day seven, he was ready.

The infiltration began at dusk. Liu's frequency mask rendered Ren invisible to the estate's array surveillance as they approached the northern gate. Xue and Feng walked through the front entrance, bloodline signatures blazing, greeting the security staff with the casual authority of family members returning from a brief absence.
"Father's in the restricted wing," Feng reported via a micro-communicator. "He's been there all day with the new channeler, working on Node 6."
"Perfect. He won't see us coming."

Ren slipped through the service entrance—the route Feng had cleared, bypassing the security checkpoints that would have flagged his presence. The frequency mask held, his dimensional signature invisible to the array sensors that monitored every square inch of the estate.
By 9 PM, they were in position: Xue and Feng at the black iron door, Ren in the service corridor beyond, Liu monitoring from a van outside the estate's perimeter.
"Bloodline access confirmed," Xue whispered. "The door recognizes us."
"Open it."
The black iron door sighed open. The corridor stretched before them—dark, ancient, humming with the containment frequency that the cascade was restoring one fraction of a percent at a time.
They went in.
One thousand and nine days.
